passage.text:
IELTS Reading Practice
Science & Technology
10 Reading Passages  ·  500–600 words each  ·  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)

Contents
Passage 1: Artificial Intelligence in Everyday Life
Passage 2: Space Exploration — Past, Present, and Future
Passage 3: Robots — How They Work and Where They Are Used
Passage 4: The Internet — Connecting the World
Passage 5: Genetic Science — Understanding Our DNA
Passage 6: Electric Cars — The Future of Transport
Passage 7: 3D Printing — Building Objects Layer by Layer
Passage 8: Smartphones — Small Devices, Big Impact
Passage 9: Climate Technology — Fighting Global Warming with Science
Passage 10: Social Media — Good and Bad for Society

Passage 1: Artificial Intelligence in Everyday Life
Topic: Artificial Intelligence  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Artificial intelligence, or AI, is a type of technology that allows computers to do things that normally need human thinking. For example, AI can understand speech, recognise faces in photos, translate languages, and make decisions. Most people use AI every day without realising it. When you ask a voice assistant like Siri a question, when a music app suggests songs you might like, or when your email moves unwanted messages to a spam folder, you are using AI. In recent years, AI has improved very quickly. This is mainly because computers have become much faster and because there is now a huge amount of data available to train AI systems.
Paragraph B
One of the most important types of AI is called machine learning. Instead of following rules that a programmer writes, a machine learning system learns by looking at large amounts of data. For example, if you show a machine learning system thousands of photos of cats and dogs, it will learn to tell the difference between them. Over time, it gets better and better at this task. A more advanced version of machine learning, called deep learning, can do more complex tasks — such as translating text, writing stories, or identifying diseases in medical scans. Deep learning systems are modelled loosely on the way the human brain works, using layers of connected units that process information.
Paragraph C
AI is being used in many different areas to help people. In hospitals, AI systems can look at medical images and find signs of cancer or other diseases at an early stage. This can help save lives because early treatment is usually more effective. In schools, AI programmes can give students personalised lessons, adjusting the difficulty to match each student's level. In factories, robots controlled by AI can build cars or sort products much faster than humans. In transport, some car companies are testing vehicles that can drive themselves using AI to read the road and avoid danger. AI is also being used to help scientists analyse large amounts of research data more quickly.
Paragraph D
However, AI also causes concern for many people. One worry is jobs. If machines can do more and more tasks, some people are afraid that many jobs will disappear. Experts disagree about how serious this problem will be — some believe new jobs will appear to replace the old ones, while others think the change will happen too fast for people to adapt. Another concern is fairness. AI systems learn from data, and if that data contains unfair ideas — for example, favouring certain groups of people over others — the AI may repeat these unfair patterns. There are also concerns about privacy, as AI systems that use cameras and personal data could be used to watch and track people. Many governments are now discussing how to create rules to make AI safer and fairer.

Passage 2: Space Exploration — Past, Present, and Future
Topic: Space Exploration  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Space exploration has been one of the greatest achievements of modern science. For thousands of years, humans could only look at the stars from the ground. This changed in the twentieth century. In 1957, the Soviet Union launched Sputnik — the first man-made satellite to orbit the Earth. A few years later, in 1961, Soviet astronaut Yuri Gagarin became the first human to travel to space. The most famous moment came in 1969, when American astronaut Neil Armstrong became the first person to walk on the Moon. These events showed that humans could travel beyond the Earth and explore space directly. Since then, scientists have sent spacecraft to all eight planets in our solar system and have made many important discoveries.
Paragraph B
For many years, space exploration was only carried out by government organisations, such as NASA in the United States or the Russian space agency. These organisations spent enormous amounts of money to build rockets and spacecraft. However, in recent years, private companies have started to play a big role in space. Companies like SpaceX, founded by Elon Musk, and Blue Origin, founded by Jeff Bezos, have built their own rockets. SpaceX became the first private company to send astronauts to the International Space Station. Private companies have also started offering space tourism — paying passengers can travel to the edge of space for a short trip. This was once something only trained astronauts could do.
Paragraph C
Space exploration has brought many benefits to people on Earth, even for those who never think about space. Satellites in orbit around the Earth are used every day for weather forecasting, GPS navigation, television, phone calls, and internet connections. Technologies developed for space missions have also found uses in everyday life. For example, memory foam — which is now used in mattresses and pillows — was originally developed for spacecraft seats. Cameras in modern smartphones use technology that was first created for space telescopes. Scientists also study the Earth from space to monitor climate change, track storms, and observe changes in rainforests and ice caps.
Paragraph D
Looking to the future, space agencies and private companies have ambitious plans. NASA plans to return humans to the Moon in the coming years as part of its Artemis programme. One of the most exciting long-term goals is sending humans to Mars. SpaceX has said it hopes to send people to Mars within the next ten to twenty years. Living on Mars would be extremely difficult — the planet has very thin air, no liquid water on its surface, and temperatures that can drop to minus 80 degrees Celsius. Scientists are working on solutions, including ways to grow food on Mars and build shelters that can protect people from the harsh environment. Whether humans will actually live on another planet in the future remains to be seen, but many people believe it is possible.

Passage 3: Robots — How They Work and Where They Are Used
Topic: Robotics  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
A robot is a machine that can carry out tasks automatically. Some robots follow a fixed set of instructions and always do the same thing. Others are more advanced and can use sensors and cameras to understand their surroundings and make decisions. Robots come in many shapes and sizes. Some look like metal arms and are used in factories. Others are designed to look and move like humans. Some are very small and can travel through narrow spaces. Others are large machines used in construction or farming. The word "robot" was first used in a play written by Czech writer Karel Capek in 1920. In that play, robots were machines that worked for humans. Today, real robots do exactly that.
Paragraph B
Robots are most commonly found in factories. Car factories, for example, use large robot arms to weld metal parts together, paint car bodies, and fit windows and doors. These robots can work faster than human workers, make fewer mistakes, and can work all day and night without getting tired. In recent years, robots have also appeared in warehouses — large storage buildings where products are kept before being sent to shops or customers. Companies like Amazon use robots to move shelves of products to human workers who then pack them into boxes. This makes the process much faster and more efficient.
Paragraph C
Robots are also used in places that are dangerous or difficult for humans to enter. For example, robots are sent into nuclear power stations to check for damage and make repairs, because the level of radiation inside is too high for humans. In the military, robots are used to investigate suspicious objects that might be bombs, without putting soldiers at risk. Underwater robots — called remotely operated vehicles, or ROVs — are used to explore the deep ocean, repair underwater pipes and cables, and study sea creatures that live in very deep water. In disaster situations, such as after an earthquake or a building collapse, small robots can be sent into spaces that are too small or dangerous for rescue workers to enter.
Paragraph D
Many people worry that robots will take away human jobs. It is true that robots have already replaced some types of jobs — particularly simple, repetitive tasks in factories. However, robots have also created new jobs. Someone needs to build the robots, program them, and fix them when they break. Many experts believe that as robots take over routine tasks, humans will be freed to do more creative and skilled work. Some governments and organisations are investing in training programmes to help workers learn new skills that work alongside robots. The key challenge for the future is making sure that the benefits of robotics are shared fairly, and that workers who lose their jobs because of automation are given the support and training they need.

Passage 4: The Internet — Connecting the World
Topic: Digital Technology  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
The internet is one of the most important inventions of the modern age. It is a huge global network that connects billions of computers and devices, allowing people to share information, communicate, and access services from almost anywhere in the world. The internet was originally developed in the 1960s by the United States military as a way to share information between computers at different locations. It became available to the general public in the early 1990s, and since then it has grown incredibly fast. Today, more than five billion people around the world use the internet — that is over 60% of the global population. In many countries, the internet is now considered as important as electricity or running water.
Paragraph B
The internet has changed the way people communicate. In the past, if you wanted to speak to someone far away, you had to make an expensive phone call or write a letter that could take days or weeks to arrive. Today, email, video calls, and messaging apps allow people to communicate instantly and for free, no matter where they are in the world. Social media platforms such as Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok allow people to share photos, videos, and opinions with friends, family, and even strangers across the world. Billions of people use these platforms every day. The internet has also changed the way people access news and entertainment — most people now read news online, and streaming services like Netflix and YouTube have replaced traditional television for many viewers.
Paragraph C
The internet has also transformed many industries. Shopping online has become very popular — people can now buy almost anything from their phone or computer and have it delivered to their door within a day or two. Banking, paying bills, and booking travel can all be done online without visiting a bank or travel agent. Education has also been transformed — students can take online courses and access educational resources from universities around the world. During the COVID-19 pandemic, millions of students and workers switched to online learning and working from home. While this was challenging for many people, it showed that much more can be done online than many people had previously thought possible.
Paragraph D
Despite its many benefits, the internet also has negative effects. Cybercrime — criminal activity carried out online — is a growing problem. Hackers can steal personal information, money, or business data. Scammers trick people into giving away their passwords or bank details. Young people are particularly at risk from online dangers, including contact with strangers, exposure to harmful content, and cyberbullying — when someone is bullied or harassed online. Spending too much time online, particularly on social media, has been linked to increased feelings of anxiety and low mood, especially in teenagers. There is also a serious problem called the "digital divide" — the gap between people who have access to the internet and those who do not. In many poorer countries, large numbers of people still have no internet access at all.

Passage 5: Genetic Science — Understanding Our DNA
Topic: Biology & Genetics  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Inside nearly every cell in the human body, there is a substance called DNA. DNA is like an instruction manual that tells the body how to grow, develop, and function. It is made up of long strings of chemical "letters" arranged in a specific order. This order of letters is called the genetic code. The complete set of DNA in a person — all their genetic information — is called their genome. The human genome contains around 20,000 to 25,000 genes. Genes are sections of DNA that carry instructions for specific things — for example, the colour of your eyes, your blood type, or how tall you are likely to grow. Children inherit half of their genes from their mother and half from their father, which is why family members often look similar.
Paragraph B
For many years, scientists wanted to read the complete human genome — to find out the exact order of all the chemical letters in human DNA. This enormous project, called the Human Genome Project, began in 1990 and involved scientists from six countries working together. It was completed in 2003. At the time, it was considered one of the greatest scientific achievements in history. When it was finished, sequencing one human genome cost about 100 million dollars and took years. Today, thanks to advances in technology, the same task can be done in just a few hours for less than 1,000 dollars. This has allowed scientists to study the genomes of millions of people and learn much more about how genes affect health and disease.
Paragraph C
Understanding genes has been very useful in medicine. Scientists have discovered that some diseases are caused by problems in a person's DNA. For example, a disease called cystic fibrosis is caused by a fault in one specific gene. Other conditions, such as heart disease and some types of cancer, are partly caused by genes but also depend on a person's lifestyle and environment. Genetic testing can now tell people whether they carry genes that increase their risk of developing certain diseases. This information can help people and their doctors make better decisions about health checks, diet, and treatment. In the future, it may also become possible to fix faulty genes and cure diseases that are currently very difficult to treat.
Paragraph D
Genetic science also raises important questions about ethics — what is right and wrong. One concern is privacy. A person's DNA contains very personal information — not just about themselves, but also about their relatives. If this information falls into the wrong hands, it could be misused. For example, some people worry that insurance companies or employers might use genetic information to treat people unfairly. Another question is about changing genes. Scientists have developed a tool called CRISPR that can edit DNA — like cutting and pasting in a document. This could be used to cure diseases, but it could also be used to choose characteristics in babies, such as eye colour or intelligence. Most scientists and governments believe strict rules are needed to make sure this technology is used responsibly.

Passage 6: Electric Cars — The Future of Transport
Topic: Transport Technology  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
For more than a hundred years, most cars have run on petrol or diesel. These fuels are burned inside the engine to create power. However, burning petrol and diesel releases gases that pollute the air and contribute to climate change. Because of this, many governments and car companies are now turning to electric cars. An electric car runs on a battery and one or more electric motors instead of a traditional engine. It produces no exhaust fumes, which makes it much better for air quality in cities. As of 2023, there were over 40 million electric cars on the world's roads. Several countries, including the UK and Norway, have announced plans to stop selling new petrol and diesel cars within the next fifteen to twenty years.
Paragraph B
Electric cars have several advantages over petrol and diesel cars. They are cheaper to run because electricity costs less than petrol in most countries. They also need less maintenance — electric motors have fewer moving parts than traditional engines, so there is less that can go wrong. For the environment, electric cars produce no direct pollution from their exhaust. This is particularly helpful in cities, where air pollution from traffic is a major health problem. Some countries generate most of their electricity from clean sources like wind and solar power, which means that electric cars in those countries produce very little pollution overall. Electric cars are also much quieter than petrol cars, which helps to reduce noise pollution in towns and cities.
Paragraph C
However, electric cars also have some disadvantages. The biggest problem for many drivers is range — how far the car can travel on a single charge. Most electric cars can travel between 200 and 400 kilometres before they need to be recharged. This is enough for most everyday journeys, but some drivers worry about running out of power on longer trips. Charging an electric car also takes much longer than filling a petrol car with fuel. Even with a fast charger, it can take 30 minutes or more to charge a car enough to continue a long journey. The number of public charging points is growing, but in many areas there are still not enough of them. The cost of buying an electric car is also higher than buying a similar petrol car, though prices are slowly falling.
Paragraph D
Governments around the world are trying to encourage more people to buy electric cars. Many countries offer financial help — such as lower taxes or cash payments — to people who buy electric vehicles. Some governments are investing in building more public charging stations to make it easier to charge cars away from home. Car companies are also working hard to improve the technology. Battery technology is getting better every year, meaning electric cars can travel further and charge faster than before. Some companies are developing batteries that can be charged to 80% in just ten minutes. As these improvements continue and prices fall, electric cars are expected to become the most common type of vehicle on the road within the next twenty to thirty years.

Passage 7: 3D Printing — Building Objects Layer by Layer
Topic: Manufacturing Technology  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
3D printing is a process that creates a real, solid object from a digital design. Instead of cutting a shape out of a block of material, a 3D printer builds the object by adding material layer by layer, from the bottom up. This is why it is sometimes called "additive manufacturing." The technology was first developed in the 1980s. For many years, 3D printers were very expensive and were mostly used by large companies to make models of new products. In recent years, however, the cost of 3D printers has dropped significantly, and smaller businesses and even individuals can now own one. There are many different types of 3D printers that use different materials, including plastic, metal, and even food.
Paragraph B
3D printing is now used in many industries. In medicine, doctors can use 3D printers to create models of a patient's bones or organs before a difficult operation. These models help the surgeon plan the operation and reduce the risk of mistakes. 3D printing can also be used to make custom artificial limbs — arms or legs made to fit a specific patient. These are much cheaper to produce than traditional artificial limbs. In the aerospace industry, companies like NASA and SpaceX use 3D printing to make parts for rockets and spacecraft. 3D-printed parts can be made more quickly and are often lighter than parts made using traditional methods. In fashion and design, 3D printing is used to create jewellery, shoes, and even furniture.
Paragraph C
One of the most exciting uses of 3D printing is in construction. In some countries, 3D printers have been used to print entire houses. A large printer moves along tracks and pushes out concrete layer by layer to build the walls. This method is faster and cheaper than traditional building methods and produces less waste. A small house can be printed in just one or two days. This technology could be very useful in countries where there is a shortage of affordable housing. It could also be used in disaster zones to quickly build shelters for people who have lost their homes. Some companies are even developing plans to use 3D printing to build structures on the Moon or Mars for future space missions.
Paragraph D
Like any technology, 3D printing also brings some challenges. One concern is that it makes it easy to copy products. If someone has the digital design for a product, they can print copies of it at home, which could mean that the original maker does not get paid. This is a problem for intellectual property — the rights that creators have over their work. Another concern is safety. In some countries, people have used 3D printers to make plastic guns that cannot be detected by metal detectors. This has worried law enforcement officials. Finally, some 3D printing materials can release harmful substances into the air when heated, so it is important to use 3D printers in well-ventilated spaces. Despite these challenges, most experts believe the benefits of 3D printing are much greater than the risks.

Passage 8: Smartphones — Small Devices, Big Impact
Topic: Digital Technology  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
The smartphone is one of the most significant inventions of the past thirty years. A smartphone is a mobile phone that can connect to the internet and run many different applications, called apps. The first smartphone that most people recognise as modern was the Apple iPhone, launched in 2007. Before smartphones, people used separate devices for different things — a phone to make calls, a camera to take photos, a map to find directions, and a computer to send emails. The smartphone combined all of these functions into one small device that fits in a pocket. Today, there are around 6.5 billion smartphone users in the world — more than 80% of the global population.
Paragraph B
Smartphones have changed how people spend their time every day. Most people now use their phone to check social media, watch videos, play games, and read news. Many people find it difficult to go even a short time without checking their phone. Research has shown that the average person looks at their smartphone around 150 times a day. Smartphones have also changed how people shop. Mobile shopping apps allow people to buy products with just a few taps on the screen. In many countries, paying for things in shops using a smartphone is now more popular than using cash or a card. Maps and navigation apps have made it much easier to find places — most people no longer carry a paper map or ask for directions.
Paragraph C
Smartphones have had a big impact on education and work. Students can use their phones to access educational apps, search for information online, and watch instructional videos. During the COVID-19 pandemic, smartphones and tablets were essential for students who had to study from home. For many workers, a smartphone means they can work from anywhere — answering emails, joining video calls, and accessing work documents even when they are not in the office. However, smartphones in the workplace can also be a distraction. Many employers have introduced rules about phone use during working hours because employees often spend time on personal apps instead of working.
Paragraph D
However, there are also concerns about the negative effects of smartphones, particularly on young people. Studies have found that spending too much time on social media apps can increase feelings of loneliness, low self-esteem, and anxiety. Many teenagers compare themselves to others online and feel bad about themselves as a result. Sleep is another concern — using a phone in bed before sleeping can make it harder to fall asleep, as the bright screen affects the brain. There is also the issue of distraction. Many road accidents happen because drivers use their phones while driving. Some countries have introduced heavy fines for using a phone while driving. Despite these concerns, smartphones are now considered an essential part of modern life for most people around the world.

Passage 9: Climate Technology — Fighting Global Warming with Science
Topic: Environmental Technology  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Climate change is one of the most serious problems the world faces today. It is caused mainly by greenhouse gases — especially carbon dioxide — that are released when we burn fuels such as coal, oil, and gas. These gases trap heat in the atmosphere and cause the Earth to warm up. To slow down climate change, we need to produce much less carbon dioxide. Technology is playing a very important role in helping to achieve this. Scientists and engineers are developing new technologies to produce clean energy, use energy more efficiently, and even remove carbon dioxide directly from the air. Without these technologies, it would be very difficult to reduce the amount of greenhouse gas in the atmosphere quickly enough.
Paragraph B
One of the most important clean energy technologies is solar power. Solar panels absorb energy from the sun and turn it into electricity. In recent years, the cost of solar panels has fallen dramatically — by over 90% since 2010. This means solar energy is now one of the cheapest ways to produce electricity in many countries. Wind power is another fast-growing clean energy source. Large wind turbines — machines with long blades that spin in the wind — can generate large amounts of electricity. Offshore wind farms, built in the sea where winds are stronger, can produce even more energy. Together, solar and wind power are expected to provide most of the world's electricity by 2050.
Paragraph C
Another important area of technology is energy storage. Because the sun does not always shine and the wind does not always blow, we need ways to store the electricity produced by solar and wind power for use when it is needed. Large batteries can store electricity and release it when demand is high. Battery technology is improving quickly, and the cost of batteries has also fallen significantly in recent years. Electric vehicles also use large batteries, and some scientists believe that millions of electric car batteries connected to the electricity network could be used to store extra energy when it is not needed and release it during busy times. Hydrogen — a gas that can be made from water using electricity — is another promising way to store energy.
Paragraph D
Some scientists are working on technologies to remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere — a process sometimes called "carbon capture." One method involves growing large numbers of trees, since trees absorb carbon dioxide as they grow. Another method uses special machines that pull air through filters and capture the carbon dioxide inside. The captured gas can then be stored underground permanently. This technology, called Direct Air Capture, already exists, but it is currently very expensive. Other ideas include adding certain minerals to the ocean to help it absorb more carbon dioxide naturally. Most climate experts agree that reducing the amount of carbon dioxide we produce is the most important step, but that carbon capture technologies will also need to play a role in solving the climate problem.

Passage 10: Social Media — Good and Bad for Society
Topic: Digital Technology & Society  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Social media refers to websites and apps that allow people to create and share content and communicate with others. Well-known examples include Facebook, Instagram, YouTube, TikTok, and X, which was formerly called Twitter. Social media has grown very quickly since the early 2000s. Today, more than five billion people — about 63% of the world's population — use social media. On average, people spend more than two hours a day on social media platforms. For many people, social media has become one of the main ways they get news, stay in contact with friends and family, and spend their free time. It has become a major part of modern life for billions of people around the world.
Paragraph B
Social media has many positive effects on society. It makes it easy to stay in touch with friends and family, even if they live far away. It has helped people find others who share their interests — for example, joining online groups for hobbies, health conditions, or local communities. Social media has also given ordinary people a platform to share their ideas, start businesses, and become well-known. Many small businesses use social media to advertise their products for free, reaching thousands of potential customers. Social media has also played an important role in social movements — campaigns for equality, justice, and human rights have often started or grown much faster because of social media. It has helped connect people who want to make a positive change in the world.
Paragraph C
However, social media also has significant negative effects. A major problem is the spread of false information, sometimes called "fake news." On social media, false stories can spread very quickly because people share things without checking if they are true. This has caused serious harm — for example, false information about health has persuaded some people not to take vaccines. Social media platforms show users content that matches their existing opinions, which means people are less likely to see different points of view. This can make people more fixed in their beliefs and less willing to understand others. Experts call this the "echo chamber" effect. Negative comments and online harassment — called cyberbullying — are also serious problems, particularly for young people.
Paragraph D
There are growing concerns about the effect of social media on mental health, especially among teenagers. Research has found that spending a lot of time on social media is linked to higher levels of anxiety and depression in young people. One reason is that social media often shows only the best and most exciting parts of people's lives, which can make others feel that their own lives are boring or not good enough. The design of social media apps also encourages people to keep scrolling and coming back — features like "likes" and notifications are designed to keep users engaged for as long as possible. Governments in several countries are now considering laws to protect young people from the harmful effects of social media, such as age limits and restrictions on how long children can use certain apps.
